```python import numpy as np import scipy as sp import scipy.special import probfit import pandas as pd data = pd.read_feather('test.feather').rho2.to_numpy() print(data) N,bins = np.histogram(data,bins=24,range=(0,4)) #bins = np.linspace(0,4,25) x = (bins[:-1]+bins[1:])/2 exposure = 3.8061025098100147 def cost(y0,k): global x,exposure,N T = (y0+k*x)*exposure return -2*np.sum(N*np.log(T)-T-sp.special.loggamma(N+1)) import iminuit minimizer = iminuit.Minuit(cost,errordef=1,y0=11,k=3,limit_y0=(0,None),limit_k=(0.1,None)) minimizer.migrad() minimizer.hesse() minimizer.minos() display(minimizer.fmin, minimizer.params,minimizer.merrors) minimizer.draw_mncontour("y0","k") ``` output: Test input [test.feather.zip](https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/files/5086372/test.feather.zip)

title: Compatibility of parameter tables and similar with dark themes
body:
Hi iminuit team,

I noticed that some (or all) of the table and matrix representations using latex or html have fixed background colors for a clean and convenient coloring. However, when using dark themes in jupyter lab or vscode the fixed colors are a disadvantage. As a result, white text appears on a white colored table row.

I can image two possible fixes:

1. Removing the background colors or adding a kind of switch for dark or light themes.
2. Setting the font color explicitly in the html and latex outputs and preventing unreadable combinations (One probably also has to set a background color for currently transparent table cells).

As reference, I am talking about the colors set here: https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/develop/src/iminuit/repr_html.py#L7

Example for the current version:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13698969/92901011-72c70d80-f420-11ea-836d-7d6ad7bd8c28.png)

.. if one monkey patches the colors:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13698969/92901101-84101a00-f420-11ea-9b22-beadb1b6e8e2.png)

```
import iminuit
iminuit.repr_html.backgrounds = ("background-color:#252526;", "background-color:#1E1E1E;")
iminuit.repr_html.good_style = "background-color:#415944;"
iminuit.repr_html.bad_style = "background-color:#B17E6A;"
iminuit.repr_html.warn_style = "background-color:#D2D2A4;"
```

Thank you!

body:
Hi, I am running into an issue when using `np_merrors()` with fixed parameters:

```python
import iminuit

def f(x):
    return x[0]**2 + 0.5*x[1]**2

m = iminuit.Minuit.from_array_func(
        f,
        [1.0, 1.0],
        error = [0.0, 0.1],
        fix=[True, False],
        name=["a", "b"],
        errordef=1,
    )
m.migrad()
m.minos(var="b")
print(m.merrors)
print(m.np_merrors())
```

results in

```
------------------------------------
|          |           b           |
------------------------------------
|  Error   |   -1.4    |    1.4    |
|  Valid   |   True    |   True    |
| At Limit |   False   |   False   |
| Max FCN  |   False   |   False   |
| New Min  |   False   |   False   |
------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 17, in <module>
    print(m.np_merrors())
  File "src/iminuit/_libiminuit.pyx", line 1150, in iminuit._libiminuit.Minuit.np_merrors
  File "[...]/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/util.py", line 257, in __getitem__
    v = super(MErrors, self).__getitem__(key)
KeyError: 'a'
```

I am using `iminuit` 1.4.9. This issue might be related to #459.

title: Parameters at limit not recognized
body:
Hi, #469 introduced a summary table field that warns about parameters fitted at their limits. The following example does not produce a warning:

```python
import iminuit

def f(x):
    return (x - 1.2) ** 2

m = iminuit.Minuit(f, limit_x=(-1, 1), errordef=1, print_level=1)
m.migrad()
print(m.values)
print(f"at limit: {m.fmin.has_parameters_at_limit}")
```

with `iminuit` 1.5.0:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
│ FCN = 0.04001                    │        Ncalls = 21 (21 total)        │
│ EDM = 1.14e-05 (Goal: 0.0002)    │               up = 1.0               │
├───────────────┬──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Valid Minimum │ Valid Parameters │        No Parameters at limit        │
├───────────────┴──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Below EDM goal          │           Below call limit           │
├───────────────┬──────────────────┼───────────┬─────────────┬────────────┤
│   Hesse ok    │  Has Covariance  │ Accurate  │  Pos. def.  │ Not forced │
└───────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────┴─────────────┴────────────┘
<ValueView of Minuit at 7f9a054bc740>
  x: 0.9999714990887609
at limit: False
```

The best-fit result of `x` should be 1.2, as confirmed by changing the limit to `(-1, 2)`.

This does not seem intended, unless I misunderstand the purpose of `has_parameters_at_limit`.

title: bug: missing python_requires
body:
`python_requires` is missing in `setup.cfg` or `setup.py`. This, for example, produces a bad error message in the case of https://github.com/scikit-hep/scikit-hep/pull/112 where iminuit 1.4 is being pinned on Python 2, and also would mean that `python2 -m pip install iminuit` is now broken when it could install a working 1.3.x release. There's no simple way to fix the problem after several releases are out that I know of (short of rereleasing every Python 3-only version with a `.post1` and a `python_requires` set, and maybe deleting the non-post releases and breaking all locked environments), but for the future, `python_requires=[">=3.6"]` should immediately be added so future Python 3 versions can be dropped gracefully. (or 3.5, whatever your current minimum is).

llvmlite (Numba) learned this the hard way with the dropping of 3.5. They got 2.7 right, but didn't do it for 3.5, and as such, `pip install numba` now fails on Python 3.5. No matter what you set `numba==` to, in fact - it requires both be pinned. It was too hard to go back and patch and post-release both llvmlite and numba to fix this.

title: documentation: custom least-squares function with a number of parameters > 2 and error (Poisson)
body:
Dear all,

1. It took a bit of time for me to implement errors for a custom least-squares function with a number of parameters > 2, a standard automatic feature of Minuit on ROOT C++, and I would suggest updating the documentation accordingly:

Reading from [the tutorial for generic LS function](https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/master/tutorial/generic_least_squares_function.ipynb) about setting n>2 parameters, I found no straightforward connection with [the basic tutorial](https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/master/tutorial/basic_tutorial.ipynb) for a custom function with standard error.

Here my code, that fuses together the two things. As a very basic user, I think could be a nice ready-made recipe in the tutorial.

```
# everything in iminuit is done through the Minuit object, so we import it
from iminuit import Minuit
# we also need a cost function to fit and import the LeastSquares function
from iminuit.cost import LeastSquares
from iminuit.util import describe, make_func_code
import traceback

class LeastSquares:
    """
    Generic least-squares cost function with error.
    """
    def __init__(self, model, x, y, err):
        self.model = model  # model predicts y for given x
        self.x = np.array(x)
        self.y = np.array(y)
        self.err = np.array(err)
 
    def __call__(self, *par):  # we accept a variable number of model parameters
        ym = self.model(self.x, *par)
        z = (self.y - ym)/(self.err**2)
        return np.sum(z**2)

class BetterLeastSquares(LeastSquares):
    def __init__(self, model, x, y, err):
        super().__init__(model, x, y, err)
        self.func_code = make_func_code(describe(model)[1:])

def PECD6(x, b1, b2, b3, b4, b5, b6):
    return ((b1*x + 0.5*b3*(5*x**3-3*x) +0.125*b5*(63*x**5-70*x**3+15*x)) / (1 + 0.5*b2*(3*x**2-1) + 0.125*b4*(35*x**4-30**2+3) + 0.0625*b6*(231*x**6-315*x**4+105*x**2-5)))

x_data = ctheta_cred
y_data = cPECD[0]
y_err = errPECD[0]

lsq = BetterLeastSquares(PECD6, x_data, y_data, y_err)

m = Minuit(lsq, limit_b1=(-1, 1))
m.migrad()
```

Please let me know if the code could be improved.

2. At [the basic tutorial page](https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/github/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/master/tutorial/basic_tutorial.ipynb) I found confusing the definition of the `custom_least_squares(a, b)` at the very top of the tutorial and its use way down at the cell number 20. Maybe this part
```
# a simple least-squares cost function looks like this...
def custom_least_squares(a, b):
    ym = line(data_x, a, b)
    z = (data_y - ym) / data_yerr ** 2
    return np.sum(z ** 2)
```
could be simply moved down to simplify the comprehension.

I hope you find these suggestions helpful.
Thank you very much for your **GREAT** job!

title: Migrad leads to crash (for some points in parameter space) after updating from v1.5.1 to v1.5.2
body:
After updating iminuit from v1.5.1 to v1.5.2, migrad (used in a likelihood for data analysis) leads to a crash for some of the points in parameter space. When using v1.5.1 it works fine for the same points and the found results for those points seem reasonable. Therefore, it seems like v1.5.1 works as intended, whereas something in v1.5.2 leads to an unwanted behavior.

More precisely, what happens is that migrad ends up with nan values for some of the fit parameters, which in turn leads to a crash inside user defined functions. Interestingly, the exactly same fit (same fit parameters, starting values, ...) does not lead to nan values using v1.5.1.

Furthermore, all parameter points that work with v1.5.2 lead to exactly the same results as with v1.5.1.

The behavior is reproducible in the sense that always the same datapoints lead to nan values in v1.5.2 and the crash was observed both on a CPU cluster system as well as a standard PC. Downgrading to v1.5.1 fixes the problem (it reappears when updating again to v1.5.2).

Unluckily the likelihood is fairly involved and contains undisclosed information such that I cannot provide the full code leading to the crash and the actual error is just a consequence of the nan values and therefore also not very insightful.

Is there anything in particular that changed from v1.5.1 to v1.5.2 that can lead to nan values in the fit parameters?
If it would be helpful, I can of course provide more information.

PS: Big thank you for having created and maintaining iminuit!

title: Shows error when "from iminuit import Minuit" in Python
body:
I had installed the latest iminuit from pip install. I am unable to solve the issue as shown below even after copying the _libiminuit.pyx file into the ~/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/iminuit directory. How can the issue be solved?

EDIT: figured out that the new versions run on python3.

title: Remove latex interface
body: We should remove the latex interface `latex_matrix`, `latex_param`, `latex_initial_param`. The more general functionality can be obtained with the external `tabulate` module.

title: Add limit property
body: Just like `value`, `error`, `fixed`, we should have a `limit` property to set and remove limits.

title: Redesign of `__init__`
body:
Once we have #517 we can deprecate the awkward and error-prone keyword-based initialization in `Minuit.__init__`.

Instead of
```
Minuit(fcn, x=1, y=0, error_x=0.2, limit_x=(0, None), fix_y=True)
```
you would do
```
m = Minuit(fcn, x=1, y=0)
m.limits["x"] = (0, None)
m.errors["x"] = 0.2
m.fixed["y"] = True
```
While this is more manual typing, it is more straight forward programming interface when using Minuit as a backend.

The other keywords `errordef`, `print_level`, `throw_nan` all have defaults and can all be set with properties after `__init__` ran, so they can be removed from init as well. We can still warn if `errordef` is not set, the warning will be delayed until `migrad` or `hesse` are called for the first time.

title: Remove `Minuit.from_array_func`
body:
With #518 we can also remove `Minuit.from_array_func`.

```py
def fcn1(a, b, c): ...
   
def fcn2(par): ...

m1 = Minuit(fcn1, a=1, b=2, c=3)
m2 = Minuit(fcn2, (1, 2, 3)) # just shorthand for Minuit(fcn2, par=(1, 2, 3))
```

title: Unprotected use of multiprocessing.cpu_count()
body:
https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/2b47d07b6f4249ff4073d4be2313b07215891df6/cmake.py#L40

This could throw and is currently unprotected. See https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.cpu_count . Instead, maybe use `os.cpu_count()`, new in 3.4+: https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/os.html#os.cpu_count which returns None instead if undeterminable. 

Suggestion:

```python
njobs = self.parallel or multiprocessing.cpu_count() or 1
```

title: Add gaussian soft-constraints to Minuit
body: While these could be handled inside the user-defined likelihood, we could also add such a constraint on the level of Minuit, to make it easy to turn it on and off.

title: check Minos limits
body: Minos limits are off in Hesse/Minos tutorial, need to investigate

title: Segmentation fault when running HESSE without any free parameters
body:
Hi, I ran into an edge case where a segmentation fault is triggered when trying to run HESSE without any free parameters:

```python
import iminuit

def func(pars):
    return (pars[0]-2)**2

m = iminuit.Minuit.from_array_func(func, [1], errordef=1, fix=[True])
m.migrad()
m.hesse()
```

This segfaults with `iminuit` 1.5.4. I am not sure whether v2 addresses this, but it would be convenient if this could get caught with an error message.

title: precision should be fcn attribute, not passed to migrad method
body: The precision like errordef is a property of the function to be minimized and so the information should be queried from the function instead of passed by a keyword to migrad, simplex and possibly other minimization algorithms.

title: Add Python-3.9 binary wheels
body: Missing numba and jax only affects the tutorials, which simply should not be tested on Python-3.9.

title: Unexpected output when running MINOS
body:
Hi, I noticed some unexpected stdout output when calling MINOS:

```python
from iminuit import Minuit

def fcn(x, y, z):
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2

fcn.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES

m = Minuit(fcn, x=0, y=0, z=0)
m.migrad()
m.minos(var="x")
```
prints:
```
return Minos error -9.09851e-10  , 9.10173e-10
```
This is with `iminuit` 1.5.4. I could not get the 2.0 RC to compile immediately, so this might already have been fixed - sorry for the issue if that is the case!

title: Allow accessing multiple parameters with simple wildcard patterns
body:
It would be nice to add support for the following:
```py
def fcn(b0, b1, b2, sig_amp, sig_center, sig_sigma):
    [...]

m = Minuit(fcn, ...)

# select multiple parameters with wildcards, ? matches one character, * matches many
m.fixed["b?"] = True
m.fixed["sig_*"] = False

# allow parameter names also in slices
m.values["sig_amp":] # selects sig_amp and all later parameters
m.values[0:"sig_amp"] # selects parameters before sig_amp
```

title: Some versions of MSVC not supported
body:
The following:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/f12cd519aa08e244ad71f56fe7a97d42117196e4/cmake_ext.py#L37

is not supported by conda-forge, I get:

```
  CMake Error at CMakeLists.txt:3 (project):
    Generator
      Visual Studio 15 2017 Win64
    does not support platform specification, but platform
      x64
    was specified.
```

The problem is that Conda-forge sets the generator for you, so trying to assume and set things breaks it. If you check for and avoid setting this if `CMAKE_GENERATOR` is set, then I think this will work.

See the `contains_arch` code here: https://github.com/pybind/cmake_example/blob/master/setup.py

title: Behavior when MINOS finds better minimum than MIGRAD
body:
Hi, I found the following in the [ROOT 6.22 release notes](https://root.cern/doc/v622/release-notes.html):

> **Minuit2**
> - Fix the case when a new better minimum is found while running MINOS

These two PRs are related: https://github.com/root-project/root/pull/4782, https://github.com/root-project/root/pull/6060.

Is this implemented in `iminuit`? If I understand the description in the PRs correctly, this would mean that if I run something like this

```python
m = Minuit(...)
m.migrad()
minimum_x = m.values["x"]
```

and then for some reason MINOS would find a better minimum

```python
m.minos("x")
```

would that cause MIGRAD to re-run (and would I need to possibly manually run HESSE?), but in any case even if MIGRAD runs would I need to re-read the best-fit values?

```python
minimum_x = m.values["x"]
```

How does `iminuit` currently behave in this scenario?

I imagine this scenario is very rare, but it seems like something that may cause unexpected behavior and I would like to catch it in code that uses `iminuit` if possible.

title: Tests missing from PyPI source tarball for version 2.0.0
body:
Tests are missing from the [PyPI source tarball](https://pypi.org/project/iminuit/#files) for version 2.0.0. I don't know if this is by intention, simply pointing out since previous versions did have the tests dir in them.

Having tests in the PyPI tarball helps when packaging iminuit into rpms for openSUSE, as we run the tests while building; for now I am downloading the tests from this GH repo, but that's an extra step I would rather avoid if possible.

Thanks for the great library. btw.

title: Pretty-printing to terminal no longer working in iminuit 2.0.0?
body:
Hi Hans,
thanks a lot for releasing iminuit 2, which comes with an even better interface than the older versions!

I encountered a problem when switching to version 2:

Printing the fval with `print(minuit.fval)` in a python script run in a terminal now gives, e.g.
```
<FMin edm=0.00013130999672212104 edm_goal=0.1 errordef=1.0 fval=35.01547206930391 has_accurate_covar=True has_covariance=True has_made_posdef_covar=False has_parameters_at_limit=False has_posdef_covar=True has_reached_call_limit=False has_valid_parameters=True hesse_failed=False is_above_max_edm=False is_valid=True nfcn=254 ngrad=0>
```

whereas in v1, I got nicely formatted output:

```
------------------------------------------------------------------
| FCN = 35.02                   |     Ncalls=254 (254 total)     |
| EDM = 0.000131 (Goal: 0.0002) |            up = 1.0            |
------------------------------------------------------------------
|  Valid Min.   | Valid Param.  | Above EDM | Reached call limit |
------------------------------------------------------------------
|     True      |     True      |   False   |       False        |
------------------------------------------------------------------
| Hesse failed  |   Has cov.    | Accurate  | Pos. def. | Forced |
------------------------------------------------------------------
|     False     |     True      |   True    |   True    | False  |
------------------------------------------------------------------
```

title: util.describe fails for certain functools.partial functions
body:
I recently came across an issue were I tried to use [`functools.partial`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.partial) to simplify my function. This error only happened when using `*args` or `**kwargs` in the partial function.

### Quick Example

This might not be a very practical example, as the minimum is rather trivial, but one could construct a reasonably difficult function that works the same way. If you are interested in a more real-world example, I'd be happy to share my code which originally lead to this issue.

```python
import numpy as np
from iminuit import Minuit
from functools import partial


def foo(x, *args):
    arguments = np.array(args)
    return np.sum(x - arguments, axis=0)


p_foo = partial(foo, 42)  # x = 42


# Minimum is trivial (should be at args[0] = 0.0 and args[1] = 0.0)
m = Minuit(p_foo, 0.04, 0.02)  # Fails!
```

More specifically, the error is:
```text
AttributeError: 'functools.partial' object has no attribute '__name__'
```
caused by this line:
https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/2b7b4a815629a8ebc97109a2e46887b8139c7715/src/iminuit/util.py#L877

### Cause of this Issue

When the keyword argument `name` is not passed to the `Minuit` constructor, the method [`iminuit.util.describe(callable)`](https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/2b7b4a815629a8ebc97109a2e46887b8139c7715/src/iminuit/util.py#L773) is used to determine the argument names from the functions signature.
In this function, we have the following three functions that help us to retrieve the argument names:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/2b7b4a815629a8ebc97109a2e46887b8139c7715/src/iminuit/util.py#L833-L835

The first will fail as a `partial` object has no attribute `func_code`. The second (`_arguments_from_inspect`) usually works fine with `partial` objects as `inspect.signature` returns the correct signature. In this case this would be `("*args",)`. However, `*args` and `**kwargs` are ignored as for these types, we construct our own argument names (`x0`, `x1`, ...). So we will eventually end up in the last function `_arguments_from_docstring` which will fail because there is no attribute `__name__` associated with a partial object. Thus, an exception is raised.

Note that this is not solved by using the `func` attribute that is provided by the partial object. This whole function is not going to work with partial functions as `inspect.getdoc` will always return the following:
```text
partial(func, *args, **keywords) - new function with partial application\nof the given arguments and keywords.
```

### Possible Solution

`_arguments_from_inspect` worked perfectly for the partial function. It got `("*args",)` and returned `()` as it should do. I would suggest adding a `try: ... except: return ()` clause around this part:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/2b7b4a815629a8ebc97109a2e46887b8139c7715/src/iminuit/util.py#L877

I would be happy to create a pull request (this would mark my first contribution to this project, how exciting!) but I'm open for ideas!

title: Improved support for simultaneous fitting
body:
A nice feature of probfit is the `SimultaneousFit` class (https://probfit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#simultaneous-fit). 

It appears that iminuit itself is already mostly capable of the features that `SimultaneousFit` provides (through its `CostSum` class). However, unless I am mistaken, there are a few convenience methods missing that would complete this feature. As probfit is deprecated, I would like to contribute this additional functionality to iminuit.

My motivation for this is my single-photoelectron spectra fitting package (https://gitlab.cta-observatory.org/cta-consortium/aswg/tools/spefit), which already utilises iminuit. I was previously unaware of these existing simultaneous-fit features when originally writing this package. I would like to release a new version of `spefit` that replaces much of its own simultaneous-fit bookkeeping with that already provided by iminuit.

Here I demonstrate a version of the probfit `SimultaneousFit` example (https://probfit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyplots/costfunc/simul.py) which solely utilises iminuit to achieve the same result:

```python
from iminuit import Minuit
from iminuit.cost import UnbinnedNLL
from iminuit.util import make_func_code
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from numpy.random import randn, seed
from numba import vectorize, float64
from math import exp, sqrt, pi

SQRT2PI = sqrt(2.0 * pi)

@vectorize([float64(float64, float64, float64)], fastmath=True)
def normal_pdf(x, mean, std_deviation):
    u = (x - mean) / std_deviation
    return exp(-0.5 * u ** 2) / (SQRT2PI * std_deviation)

seed(0)
width = 2.0
data1 = randn(1000) * width + 1
data2 = randn(1000) * width + 2

normal_pdf.func_code = make_func_code(("x", "mu_1", "sigma"))
lh1 = UnbinnedNLL(data1, normal_pdf)
normal_pdf.func_code = make_func_code(("x", "mu_2", "sigma")) # TODO: Add method to rename lh1
lh2 = UnbinnedNLL(data2, normal_pdf)

simlh = lh1 + lh2

m = Minuit(simlh, mu_1=1.2, mu_2=2.2, sigma=1.5)

def plot(cost, minuit, i, ax):
    names_i = [minuit.parameters[map_i] for map_i in simlh._maps[i]]  # TODO: Add method to BasicView to obtain view subset
    values_i = [minuit.values[map_i] for map_i in simlh._maps[i]]  # TODO: Add method to BasicView to obtain view subset
    errors_i = [minuit.errors[map_i] for map_i in simlh._maps[i]]  # TODO: Add method to BasicView to obtain view subset
    data_i = cost[i].data
    model_i = cost[i]._model   # TODO: Expose public access in Cost? Unfortunately not common between Cost methods...
    bins, edges = np.histogram(data_i, bins=100)
    mid = (edges[1:] + edges[:-1]) / 2
    binwidth = (edges[1] - edges[0])
    ax.errorbar(mid, bins, np.sqrt(bins), fmt="b.", capsize=0, zorder=0)
    y = model_i(mid, *values_i) * np.sum(bins * binwidth)
    ax.plot(mid, y)
    t = ""
    for n, v, e in zip(names_i, values_i, errors_i):
        t += f"{n} = {v:.3f} ± {e:.3f}\n"
    ax.text(0.01, 0.99, t, transform=ax.transAxes, va='top')
    
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1)
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
plot(simlh, m, 0, ax1)
plot(simlh, m, 1, ax2)
fig.suptitle("Before")

m.migrad()  # fit

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1)
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
plot(simlh, m, 0, ax1)
plot(simlh, m, 1, ax2)
fig.suptitle("After")
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17825673/111623627-e50f1500-87ea-11eb-89ea-f132cef06080.png)

The TODOs highlight the areas where functionality might be added to iminuit to better support this feature. Please correct me if I have overlooked some existing features in iminuit which already provide this.

To summarise, the main features which appear to be missing are:
- Nice way to rename the func code to indicate which parameters are shared vs unique
- Access a subset of the ValueView for each individual fit.

title: Add beginner-friendly tutorial for use of cost functions
body:
I recall in C++ ROOT there was a [`TH1::Fit()`](https://root.cern.ch/doc/master/classTH1.html#a7e7d34c91d5ebab4fc9bba3ca47dabdd) flag where one could easily switch between a chi2 and a Poisson likelihood for fitting a histogram. Does something like that exist in `iminuit`? I scoured the documentation, especially for `iminuit.cost`, but the keyword "Poisson" is not present.

`iminuit.cost.BinnedNLL` looks relevant, but then do I need to provide the Poisson cdf manually? If so, a `PoissonLoss` class would be very useful to build in.

Or maybe I could just change the `loss` kwarg in `iminuit.cost.LeastSquares`?

title: Simplify merge_func_code
body:
@mbaak I merged your patch but I am not at all happy with this function. I am considering to revert this at least partially.

Is this interface needed? What is the use case for each option?
```
        - **skip_first** option boolean to skip prefixing the first argument
          of each function. This should normally be true when prefix is not
          None.

        - **factor_list** option list of functions. For function that skip_first
          should not be applied. The choice of prefix applice to factor_list
          will be in the same order as prefix. Default None

        - **skip_prefix** list of variable names that prefix should not be
          applied to.
```
factor_list should be handled by skip_first. skip_first can either be a boolean or a sequence of boolean with the same length as the list of functions to be merged. The boolean for each function then tells you to skip or not. skip_prefix should be removed.

title: BasicView should accept list of keys and return a sequence of the values
body:
@watsonjj wants in https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/issues/621 to have easy access to the parameter names and fitted values for a cost function that is part of the a CostSum. We should mimic the numpy array interface for this, which allows one to pass a list of indices to get a numpy array of the values at those key positions.

```py
m = iminuit.Minuit(..., mu=1, sigma=2, beta=3)

m.values[["mu", "beta"]] # returns [1, 3]
m.values[["beta", "sigma"]] # returns [3, 2]
# etc

# using indices should also work
m.values[[0, 2]] # returns [1, 3]
m.values[[2, 1]] # returns [3, 2]

# likewise for m.parameters, m.covariance, m.fixed
```

title: API to rename parameters
body:
It seems there is a need to rename parameters in some functions, especially when combining likelihoods (what @watsonjj calls simultaneous fits).

It is already possible to rename parameters like this:

```py
def normal_pdf(x, mu, sigma):
   # ...

def my_normal_pdf(x, mu_1, sigma_1):
      return normal_pdf(x, mu_1, sigma_1)
```

For common use this is a bit too much to type. It was proposed in #621 to handle this in the Cost function, but that is not the right place for such a functionality, since it is quite general and it should be applied to the model and not the cost.

We want something like this:
```py
# replace all parameter names
f = make_with_signature(normal_pdf, "x", "mu_1", "sigma_1")
g = make_with_signature(normal_pdf, "x", "mu_2", "sigma_2")

# renaming only some parameters
h = make_with_signature(normal_pdf, mu="mu_1") 
```

@watsonjj and @mbaak please comment.

title: repeated minos calls fail (only without having called migrad before) OLD
body:
First of all, many thanks for making minos independent of previous migrad runs as described [in the changelog](https://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/changelog.html#id8)

It seems however that this does not yet fully work. As an example the very basic script from the README, feeding the fit result into a new Minuit instance.

```
from iminuit import Minuit

def fcn(x, y, z):
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2

fcn.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES

m = Minuit(fcn, x=0, y=0, z=0)

m.migrad()  # run optimiser
m.values  # x: 2, y: 3, z: 4

m.hesse()   # run covariance estimator

m2 = Minuit(fcn, **m.values)

m2.hesse()  # works
m2.minos()  # fails (also without previous `hesse`
```

Am I maybe missing something? Or do you have an idea what is going wrong?

title: Minuit.values[0:N] and Minuit.errors[0:N] don't work as a normal list would do
body:
Hi, iminuit team.

Thanks for providing the excellent python Minuit interface. I came across an issue in iminuit 2.x. Suppose V = Minuit.values has N dimensions, I found that calling V[0:N-1] works but calling V[0:N] would inflict an index error. But for a normal N dimensional list p in python, calling p[0:N] is legit. Same problem happened for Minuit.errors. To get over the problem I needed to do a trick by V = Minuit.values[0:] instead of V = Minuit.values first, then V[0:N] can be legit. It'd be convenient if I wouldn't need to do the circumvention trick.

best.

title: [BUG] repeated minos calls fail (only without having called migrad before)
body:
As described in the title, multiple calls of minos (without any change in anything) will raise an error here (if migrad never ran):
https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/b5c3ddcfec404bbd38695a7bcd24b3f6bf367ea5/src/iminuit/minuit.py#L930


What seems weird: the number until it fails is fully deterministic _and depends on the cl chosen_. I've plotted the number of minos calls vs the cl

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17454848/113353834-8f844d80-933e-11eb-8aa8-b2d3ff628e45.png)

Here is a minimal example (taking the basic example from the docs) to reproduce the issue:

```python
from iminuit import Minuit

def fcn(x, y, z):
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2

fcn.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES

m = Minuit(fcn, x=2, y=1.3, z=1)
m.migrad()

assert m.fmin.is_valid

m2 = Minuit(fcn,
            x=m.values['x'],
            y=m.values['y'],
            z=m.values['z'])
for i in range(7):  # default cl fails at 7, 6 works
    m2.minos()
```

Using a reset instead of the error (or even ignoring the error and passing) solves the problem seemingly (but I may don't understand Minuit enough for this).

The full script to reproduce the plot.

I mentioned this issue also in #631 but it may got lost, so creating a new one here.

_Sidenote: I highly assume that this is indented behavior, at least it is not documented that an error will be raised. If that is intended, I would suggest to write that in the docs._

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

from iminuit import Minuit
import numpy as np

cl = 0.688 # Fails after three for >= 0.96


def fcn(x, y, z):
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2

fcn.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES  # also fails for LIKELIHOOD

cls = np.linspace(0, 1, 1000)[1:-1]
do_print = False
fails_after = []
for cl in cls:

    m = Minuit(fcn, x=2, y=1.3, z=1)
    m.strategy = 1  # fails for all strategies 0, 1 and 2
    m.print_level = 0

    m.migrad()  # run optimiser
    assert m.fmin.is_valid

    m2 = Minuit(fcn,
                x=m.values['x'],
                y=m.values['y'],
                z=m.values['z'])

    # m2.migrad()  # "SOLUTION" 1: Using this line makes it work (but changes maybe the minimum?

    # m2.hesse()  # calling hesse (which works) before or anywhere or multiple times does not help
    try:
        for i in range(30):  # try often enough to check if it fails
            i += 1
            # m2.reset()  # SOLUTION 2: Resetting before the MINOS call (with a new minimum)
            m2.minos(cl=cl)
    except RuntimeError as error:
        if 'Function minimum is not valid.' in error.args[0]:
            fails_after.append(i)
            if do_print:
                print(f'REPEATED MINOS CALL FAILED during {i} trials.'
                      f' The error was raised with cl={cl}: {error}')
    else:
        if do_print:
            print(f'The error was NOT raised with {i} trials and cl={cl}.'
                  f' It seems we can now safely call "MINOS" again'
                  f' as much as we like.' )

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot(cls, fails_after)
plt.xlabel("Confidence Level")
plt.ylabel("Failed at trial number")
plt.show()


</details>

title: Provided gradient not being used?
body:
Hi iminuit team!

I am using iminuit v2.4.0 with Python 3.8.5 to minimize a function where I also know the gradient (it's automatically differentiated using `jax`). Schematically, my code looks like this:

```python
from jax import grad
from iminuit import Minuit

def negative_log_prob(x):
    # ...
    return chi2 / 2

negative_dlog_prob = grad(negative_log_prob)

x0 = ...
limits = ...

m = Minuit(negative_log_prob, x0, grad=negative_dlog_prob)
m.limits = limits
m.errordef = Minuit.LIKELIHOOD
m.strategy = 0
m.migrad(ncall=10)
```

My likelihood is quite slow to evaluate (~1 second) and I have 25 parameters. When I run migrad, it takes very long and in the log output I can see things like 

```
D MnSeedGenerator 25 free parameters, FCN pointer 0x7ffcaeef0160
D InitialGradientCalculator Calculating initial gradient at point 
[...]
D InitialGradientCalculator Computed initial gradient for parameter [...]
D InitialGradientCalculator Computed initial gradient for parameter [...]
[...]
D InitialGradientCalculator Computed initial gradient for parameter [...]
D Numerical2PGradientCalculator Assumed precision eps 8.88178e-16 eps2 5.96046e-08
D Numerical2PGradientCalculator Calculating gradient around value 0.376557 at point 
[...]
D Numerical2PGradientCalculator  parameter cycle              x           step             f1             f2            grd             g2
D Numerical2PGradientCalculator [...]
```

So it looks like it *does* compute the gradient numerically itself rather than using the provided gradient, which takes very long due to the large number of parameters.

Is there something obvious that I could be doing wrong?

Thanks!

title: Wrong start value when using fixed variables
body:
When using "m.fixed['a'] = False", the starting value for parameter 'b' is now the starting value for parameter 'a', i.e. -17 instead of 30. 'b' has the correct starting value if using "m.fixed['a'] = True".

I'm using version 2.6.0 but it's the same in 2.4.0. Here is a small code example:

```py
from iminuit import Minuit

t = [0,1]
data = [2,5]
def cost(a,b):
    print(a,b)

    cost = 0
    for i in range(2):
        cost += pow(data[i]-(a+b*t[i]),2.0)
    return cost

m = Minuit(cost, a=-17, b=30)
m.fixed['a'] = False
m.errordef = 1
m.migrad()
print(m.params)
```

Sorry if I bother you. First of all I am using iminuit to great advantage and I am very grateful. 

I am analysing muon spin spectroscopy data with large number of DoFs & parameters, eg  when trying to perform global fits over many datasets, typically experiments varying temperature. I find  no constraint in iminuit itself, but in practice my code times out.

CUDA on GPUs would be a solution, and I am reading of cupy. https://cupy.dev/

Is this feasible? Is it in your plans? Or do you have alternative plans in mind to the same end.

Thanks a lot for any feedback.  

Best regards Roberto

title: Allow calling scipy and nlopt minimizers from Minuit object
body:
We already support mixing Minuit with other minimisers, but we do not provide a convenient interface for this. Something like this should work:
```py
def fcn(a, b, c): [...]

m = Minuit(fcn, a=1, b=2, c=3)
m.scipy(method=...) # only works if scipy is installed
# optionally run m.migrad()
m.hesse()

# OR

m = Minuit(fcn, a=1, b=2, c=3)
m.nlopt(method=...) # only works if nlopt is installed
m.hesse()
```
For this to work, scipy and nlopt have to get access to the parameter limits and only see the parameters that are not fixed. The results need to be converted into Minuits internal C++ structures.

title: Builtin cost functions potentially fail if dtype=float128 is used
body: Reported by Raphael Stock by email. If a model returns an array with dtype float128 and the builtin cost functions use Numba, an error is raised because Numba does not support this float type.

title: Add tutorial on how to use RooFit cost function with iminuit
body: Not sure if it is possible to interface the RooFit cost function with iminuit, but if it possible, we should have a tutorial about it.

title: Minuit.mncontour fails with ValueError depending on parameter order
body:
In the process of adding [support for iminuit v2.0 for Gammapy](https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/pull/3409) I came across the following bug:

```python3
import numpy as np
from iminuit import Minuit
from iminuit.cost import LeastSquares

def line(x, a, b, c):
    return a + x * b + c * x ** 2

np.random.seed(1)
data_x = np.linspace(0, 1, 10)
data_yerr = 0.1
data_y = line(data_x, 1, 2, 3) + data_yerr * np.random.randn(len(data_x))

least_squares = LeastSquares(data_x, data_y, data_yerr, line)

m = Minuit(least_squares, a=0, b=0, c=0)

m.migrad()
m.hesse()

m.mncontour(x="b", y="c") # works
m.mncontour(x="c", y="b") # fails
```
Fails with:
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-10-d39f11243df4> in <module>
     19 
     20 m.mncontour(x="a", y="b")
---> 21 m.mncontour(x="c", y="b")

~/opt/anaconda3/envs/gammapy-dev/lib/python3.7/site-packages/iminuit/minuit.py in mncontour(self, x, y, cl, size)
   1395         vary = self._free_parameters()
   1396         if x not in vary or y not in vary:
-> 1397             raise ValueError("mncontour cannot be run on fixed parameters.")
   1398 
   1399         with TemporaryErrordef(self._fcn, factor):

ValueError: mncontour cannot be run on fixed parameters.
```
But all parameters are free to vary.

From a quick look [here](https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/develop/src/iminuit/minuit.py#L1785) it seems `._free_parameters()` returns a generator, which might be empty after the first iteration checking for `x`, depending on the position of `x` in the list. So the second check for `y` must fail...

title: Normalization of BinnedNLL?
body:
I assume BinnedNLL minimizes a likelihood that is the product of Poisson terms in each bin.  The factorials in the denominators have no impact on the estimators, and presumably slow the calculation down, so they are usually omitted.  Are they included here?

I ask, because I want to write a BakerCousins likelihood ratio, where the likelihood is divided by the likelihood of a saturated data set (and the ratio should asymptotically follow a chi2), so I need to make sure both normalizations are the same.

title: Add support to release aarch64 wheels
body:
Problem
---------
On aarch64, pip install iminuit builds the wheels from source code and then install it. It requires user to have development environment installed on his system. also, it take some time to build the wheels than downloading and extracting the wheels from pypi.

Resolution
-----------
On aarch64, pip install iminuit should download the wheels from pypi

@HDembinski, Please let me know your interest on releasing aarch64 wheels. I can help in this.

title: Minuit object has an error when serialized with Pickle
body:
I've tried saving the Minuit object via pickle, but find that has an error when I try to print the object after loading it:

See the script below that generated this error


```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "testiminuit-pickle.py", line 88, in <module>
    print(pMinuit)
  File "/u/ec/roodman/.local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/minuit.py", line 1972, in __str__
    s.append(str(self.covariance))
  File "/u/ec/roodman/.local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/util.py", line 332, in __str__
    return _repr_text.matrix(self)
  File "/u/ec/roodman/.local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/_repr_text.py", line 178, in matrix
    names = tuple(arr._var2pos)
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable
```

```

import numpy as np
import pickle
from iminuit import Minuit

class testing(object):

    def __init__(self):
        self.x = np.arange(0.,100.,1.)
        self.yerr = np.random.normal(size=100)

    def yfunc(self,par):
        y = par[0] + par[1]*self.x + par[2]*self.x**2
        return y

    def mkdata(self):
        truepar = np.array([4.0,-0.4,2.0])
        self.ydata = self.yfunc(truepar) + 1.*self.yerr

    def chisq(self,par):

        # calculate chi2
        dy = self.ydata-self.yfunc(par)
        chisquared = np.sum(dy**2)
        print("chisq = ",chisquared)
        return chisquared

    def calcgrad(self,par):

        gradArr = np.zeros(par.shape[0])
        gradArr[0] = -2. * np.sum( (self.ydata-self.yfunc(par)) )
        gradArr[1] = -2. * np.sum( (self.ydata-self.yfunc(par)) * self.x )
        gradArr[2] = -2. * np.sum( (self.ydata-self.yfunc(par)) * self.x**2 )
        return gradArr


#chisq.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES

startpar = [3.,0.,1.]
names = ["a","b","c"]

atest = testing()
atest.mkdata()

gMinuit = Minuit(atest.chisq,startpar,name=names,grad=atest.calcgrad)
#gMinuit = Minuit(chisq,startpar,name=names)
gMinuit.print_level = 1 # 3 has lots more details
gMinuit.strategy = 1

# set limits...
for aname in names:
    gMinuit.limits[aname] = (-10,10.)
    gMinuit.errors[aname] = 0.01

# fix a parameter
gMinuit.fixed['a'] = True

# print setup
gMinuit.init_params

# do fit
gMinuit.migrad()

# print out results
print(gMinuit.values)
print(gMinuit.fmin)
print(gMinuit.errors)
print(gMinuit.covariance)

# release a
gMinuit.fixed['a'] = False

# do fit
gMinuit.migrad()

# print out results
print(gMinuit)
print(gMinuit.values)
print(gMinuit.fmin)
print(gMinuit.errors)
print(gMinuit.covariance)

# pickle
pickle.dump(gMinuit,open('test.pkl','wb'))

pMinuit = pickle.load(open('test.pkl','rb'))

print(pMinuit.values)
print(pMinuit)
```

body:
Something analog to

title: iminuit sometimes reports ALL OK even when errors are NaN
body: There is no minimal example to reproduce yet, but I observed cases where iminuit reports everything green, but the errors are NaN. This should not happen. It is probably an error in MnHesse, which does not check the derivatives it computes for NaNs.

title: Check support for higher-dimensional data in *BinnedNLL, LeastSquares
body:
Cost functions should support higher-dimensional data, but that is untested and probably broken.

Status of multivariate support

- [x] BinnedNLL

- [x] LeastSquares

title: Migrad vs Hesse results in `Minuit.fmin`
body:
Hi, I noticed that the fit results shown via `Minuit.fmin` include results from Hesse, even though the table only mentions Migrad at the top. Is this behavior intended?

Here is an example, I am using `iminuit` 2.9.0:
```python
from iminuit import Minuit

def fcn(x, y, z):
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y - 3) ** 2 + (z - 4) ** 2

fcn.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES

m = Minuit(fcn, x=0, y=0, z=0)
m.print_level = 1
res = m.migrad(ncall=25)
print("after MIGRAD:")
print(m.fmin)

m.hesse()
print("\nafter HESSE:")
print(m.fmin)
```

Output:
```
W MnHesse Maximum number of allowed function calls exhausted; will return diagonal matrix
W VariableMetricBuilder Invalid Hessian - exit the minimization
after MIGRAD:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                Migrad                                   │
├──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ FCN = 6.731e-18                  │              Nfcn = 27               │
│ EDM = 6.73e-18 (Goal: 0.0002)    │                                      │
├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│         INVALID Minimum          │        No Parameters at limit        │
├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Below EDM threshold (goal x 10)  │           ABOVE call limit           │
├───────────────┬──────────────────┼───────────┬─────────────┬────────────┤
│  Covariance   │     Hesse ok     │APPROXIMATE│NOT pos. def.│ Not forced │
└───────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────┴─────────────┴────────────┘

after HESSE:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                Migrad                                   │
├──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ FCN = 6.731e-18                  │              Nfcn = 43               │
│ EDM = 6.73e-18 (Goal: 0.0002)    │                                      │
├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Valid Minimum           │        No Parameters at limit        │
├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Below EDM threshold (goal x 10)  │           Below call limit           │
├───────────────┬──────────────────┼───────────┬─────────────┬────────────┤
│  Covariance   │     Hesse ok     │ Accurate  │  Pos. def.  │ Not forced │
└───────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────┴─────────────┴────────────┘
```

I used to run both Migrad and Hesse, and only check results after both of these steps, thinking issues would still be reflected in the final `Minuit.fmin` object. This is not the case, and the original failure (due to the artificial `ncall` limitation) is no longer reflected at the end.

I see the benefit in also being able to get information about how the Hesse calculation went. It was not clear to me so far that these appear in the table, given its title and the presence of other results from Migrad (FCN / EDM, which I guess have no equivalent in Hesse and therefore stay unchanged). Maybe the title of the table could reflect that it is not only Migrad information which is being shown here?

title: Allow users to chose their own grid for mncontour, mnprofile
body:
@iasonkrom wrote on Gitter:

> May I suggest a small iminuit feature that I would find useful?
I think in methods like mnprofile and mncontour It would be really nice to have the ability to scan on points that are evenly spaced on a log scale.
> I had to do this recently using iminuit because one fitting parameter was the thermal cross section for a generic WIMP. Of course I wrote a for loop myself to do this but it would be nice to have it built in since you already have the linear scanning.
> Or maybe just allow the user to choose the points as input so they can use np.logspace for that.
if that already exists and I'm completely blind and don't see it in the documentation then I'm sorry.

title: Tests do not work on i386 due to use of float128
body:
On the Linux i386 platform (at least), the data type `numpy.float128` does not exist. This causes the test `test_cost.py` to fail. I believe `float128` is being used to map to `long double` in C++. https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/10288, suggests that the data type `numpy.longdouble` should likely be used instead of `float128`, as `long double` is not an ieee128 datatype on that platform.

See https://gist.github.com/jeremysanders/9aca53cfccd5414d43f9f33eae911287 for a patch.

title: Covariance matrix compatibility with uncertainties
body:
Following the Quick start <https://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/stable/notebooks/basic_tutorial.html> I tried to use it with the uncertainties package <https://github.com/lebigot/uncertainties>. 
I encountered following error:
```py
>>> import uncertainties as unc
>>> unc.correlated_values(m.values, m.covariance)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-23-5b19c7ec7df3> in <module>
      1 import uncertainties as unc
----> 2 unc.correlated_values(m.values, m.covariance)

/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/uncertainties/core.py in correlated_values(nom_values, covariance_mat, tags)
    184             # separates back the nominal values and the standard deviations:
    185             list(zip(nom_values, std_devs)),
--> 186             covariance_mat/norm_vector/norm_vector[:,numpy.newaxis],
    187             tags)
    188 

/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/iminuit/util.py in __getitem__(self, key)
    225         var2pos = self._var2pos or {}
    226         if isinstance(key, tuple):  # tuple is special case for __getitem__
--> 227             key = tuple(var2pos.get(k, k) for k in key)
    228         elif isinstance(key, str):
    229             key = var2pos[key]

/opt/conda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/iminuit/util.py in <genexpr>(.0)
    225         var2pos = self._var2pos or {}
    226         if isinstance(key, tuple):  # tuple is special case for __getitem__
--> 227             key = tuple(var2pos.get(k, k) for k in key)
    228         elif isinstance(key, str):
    229             key = var2pos[key]

TypeError: unhashable type: 'slice'
```
I am not sure if this is intended behavior and if it requires a fix either on the iminuit or uncertainties side.
Nonetheless, the work around/solution I found for this is to avoid the custom `__getitem__` function in `Matrix` and use the original from `np.ndarray`.

```py
>>> import uncertainties as unc
>>> from iminuit.util import Matrix
>>> unc.correlated_values(m.values, super(Matrix, m.covariance))

(1.0072676945409316+/-0.058775382447305013,
 1.9660364327569773+/-0.09908674168467936)
```

title: Step size of fixed parameters changes after v2.12.1 due to heuristic being applied
body:
Hi, the step size guess heuristic in #759 is being applied to fixed parameters. This results in the error of fixed parameters changing during MIGRAD. Previously the step size was carried through without changes. This allowed for quickly filtering out fixed parameters when looking at best-fit values and errors without having to refer back to a mask specifying which parameters are fixed.

Example setup:
```python
from iminuit import Minuit


def f(x):
    return x[0] ** 2 + 0.5 * x[1] ** 2


m = Minuit(f, [1.0, 1.0])
m.fixed = [True, False]
m.errors = [0.0, 0.1]
m.migrad()
print(m.errors.to_dict())
```

with `iminuit==v2.12.1`:
```python
{'x0': 0.0, 'x1': 1.4142135622742678}
```

with `iminuit==v2.12.2` (same behavior with `v2.12.3.beta2`):
```python
[...]/iminuit/util.py:143: UserWarning: Assigned errors must be positive. Non-positive values are replaced by a heuristic.
  warnings.warn(
{'x0': 0.1, 'x1': 1.4142135622742678}```

title: Cannot pickle MaskedCost objects starting with version 2.12
body:
Hello, 

I recently updated my version of iminuit:
```
2.11.2-py310hd8f1fbe_1 --> 2.12.2-py310hd8f1fbe_0
```
and found that I was no longer able to pickle classes which inherit from MaskedCost. I think the issue is due to the [definition](https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/develop/src/iminuit/cost.py#L625) of `up()` within the `__init__` function. 

Here is a minimal code snippet to illustrate the problem. It executes without error on version `2.11.2` and does not work on `2.12.2` (and `2.14`):
```
import iminuit
import pickle 
from iminuit.cost import LeastSquares
print(f"{iminuit.__version__=}")

def func(x,p):
    return x*p[0] + p[1]

c = LeastSquares([1,2,3,4], [1,2,3,4], [1,1,1,1], func)
print(c)

with open('./test.pickle', 'wb') as fout:
    pickle.dump(c,fout)

with open("./test.pickle", 'rb') as fin:
    loaded = pickle.load(fin)
print(loaded)
```


```
>>>python --version
Python 3.10.5
>>> python bug.py 
iminuit.__version__='2.12.2'
<iminuit.cost.LeastSquares object at 0x7fbf16438040>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/jlab/github/g2_analysis/analysis/bug.py", line 13, in <module>
    pickle.dump(c,fout)
AttributeError: Can't pickle local object 'MaskedCost.__init__.<locals>.up'
```
vs.
```
>>>python bug.py 
iminuit.__version__='2.11.2'
<iminuit.cost.LeastSquares object at 0x7fce63d1bed0>
<iminuit.cost.LeastSquares object at 0x7fce62a52960>
```

Thank you!

title: Minuit.interactive outside of Jupyter notebooks
body: Minuit.interactive can detect whether it is run inside a notebook and only then generate the UI with ipywidgets. Outside of a notebook, it could generate the UI with PyQt. This would allow one to call Minuit.interactive for debugging when some fit in a long-running script fails that does a lot of fits at once.

title: UnbinnedNLL and ExtendedUnbinnedNLL: make number of bins configurable
body: `UnbinnedNLL` and `ExtendedUnbinnedNLL` use the `visualize` method in `UnbinnedCost`. The number of bins used in the visualization is currently fixed to 50, this should become user-configurable.

title: LeastSquares.visualize fails for model functions which accept arrays of parameters
body:
Hello,

After the quick bug fix regarding pickling, I was excited to upgrade to the latest version and try the new interactive fitting. However, I noticed that the `visualize()` method for the `LeastSquares` cost function only works when each parameter is an individual argument rather than a numpy array
```
def func1(x,p): #fails
    return x*p[1] + p[0]

def func2(x,p0,p1): #works great
    return x*p1 + p0
```

The traceback I get when executing this python code
```
cost1 = LeastSquares(xs,ys,np.ones_like(xs),func1)
m1 = iminuit.Minuit(cost1, [2,2])

m1.migrad()
cost1.visualize([2,2])
```
is:
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
/home/jlab/github/g2_analysis/analysis/interactive.ipynb Cell 7 in <cell line: 1>()
----> [1](vscode-notebook-cell://wsl%2Bubuntu-20.04/home/jlab/github/g2_analysis/analysis/interactive.ipynb#ch0000010vscode-remote?line=0) cost1.visualize([2,2])

File ~/miniconda3/envs/gm2/lib/python3.10/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py:1447, in LeastSquares.visualize(self, args, model_points)
   1445     ym = self.model(xm, *args)
   1446 else:
-> 1447     xm, ym = _smart_sampling(lambda x: self.model(x, *args), x[0], x[-1])
   1448 plt.plot(xm, ym)

File ~/miniconda3/envs/gm2/lib/python3.10/site-packages/iminuit/util.py:1453, in _smart_sampling(f, xmin, xmax, start, tol)
   1451 def _smart_sampling(f, xmin, xmax, start=5, tol=5e-3):
   1452     x = np.linspace(xmin, xmax, start)
-> 1453     ynew = f(x)
   1454     ymin = np.min(ynew)
   1455     ymax = np.max(ynew)

File ~/miniconda3/envs/gm2/lib/python3.10/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py:1447, in LeastSquares.visualize.<locals>.<lambda>(x)
   1445     ym = self.model(xm, *args)
   1446 else:
-> 1447     xm, ym = _smart_sampling(lambda x: self.model(x, *args), x[0], x[-1])
   1448 plt.plot(xm, ym)

TypeError: func1() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given
```

Because of this, the visualization in `Minuit.interactive()` also fails. I've uploaded a notebook [here](https://gist.github.com/jlabounty/b98b56791e2756e1fcf358eafff22489) showing the issue in more detail. 

Thanks for all the work that goes into maintaining this project!

title: docs: function name for SciPy-like interface
body:
Hi, while trying out the SciPy-like interface to `iminuit` I noticed that the docs list the relevant function as `iminuit.minimize.minimize` (https://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#iminuit.minimize.minimize). As far as I can tell, the function should be `iminuit.minimize` instead:
```python
Python 3.9.10 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Feb  1 2022, 21:25:34)
[Clang 11.1.0 ] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import iminuit
>>> iminuit.__version__
'2.15.1'
>>> iminuit.minimize
<function minimize at 0x10630d550>
>>> iminuit.minimize.minimize
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'function' object has no attribute 'minimize'
```
The function is available at the top level via
https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/2012c5da71ce26b011c85beefde3423b3979a88a/src/iminuit/__init__.py#L30
and I am guessing that Sphinx autodoc may not understand that, as it currently documents the module `iminuit.minimize` and then finds the `minimize` function inside that:

```
.. automodule:: iminuit.minimize
    :members:
```

title: Allow FixedView to be used to select other views
body:
Minuit.fixed returns an object that mimics the numpy array interface and holds boolean values. The numpy expectation is that this array can be used as a mask for other arrays, but this currently does not work.

```py
from iminuit import Minuit

m = Minuit(lambda x, y : x ** 2 + y ** 2, 1, 2)
m.fixed[0] = True

m.values[m.fixed] # returns [1, 2], not [1]
m.values[~m.fixed] # does not work, because the operator is not supported
m.values[m.fixed] = 0 # sets all values, not only those for m.fixed == 0
```
By the way, this works correctly:
```
err = np.array(m.errors)
err[m.fixed] = 0
```

title: Add a `CITATION.CFF` file
body:
That's a machine-readable format for citations which is supported by github and would add a `Cite this repository` button to the github interface. For why else it's useful see https://citation-file-format.github.io.

There's already a `CITATION.rst` file and I wouldn't remove that because I assume that for many iminuit users the sphinx/readthedocs page is their starting page, as it's the top result in google. Not sure if there's a way to integrate both without duplicating the information.

I found that scikit-hep projects the `CITATION.cff` doesn't seem widely adopted yet, so far I only found such a file for the `hist` package (but I didn't check _all_ repositories). But I think it's a good idea and would propose to push its usage.

title: Add Minuit.visualize
body:
Some cost functions can be visualized. To visualize the fitted model, one does

```py
m = Minuit(mycost, ...)
m.migrad()
mycost.visualize(m.values)
```

To simplify this, add a `visualize` method to `Minuit`, so that the following works

```py
m.visualize()  # calls mycost.visualize(m.values) if mycost.visualize exists or raises an exception
```

title: Improve error messages when using builtin cost functions
body:
There are many ways to make mistakes and use the builtin cost functions incorrectly. The user has to provide the model and it is easy to return an array of incorrect size.

```py
from iminuit import cost, Minuit
from numba_stats import norm
import numpy as np

n = [1, 2, 3]
edges = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4]

def model(x, n, a, b):
    # oopsi, returned probability per bin instead of scaled cdf
    return n * np.diff(norm.cdf(x, a, b))
    # return n * norm.cdf(x, a, b)  # correct

c = cost.ExtendedBinnedNLL(n, edges, model)
c(1, 2, 3)
```
This produces a very confusing error message:
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
/var/folders/tl/pv6mt7z17tz0stm1fjfg01cc0000gn/T/ipykernel_28667/1963231075.py in <module>
     12 
     13 c = cost.ExtendedBinnedNLL(n, edges, model)
---> 14 c(1, 2, 3)

/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py in __call__(self, *args)
    482         float
    483         """
--> 484         r = self._call(args)
    485         if self.verbose >= 1:
    486             print(args, "->", r)

/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py in _call(self, args)
   1292         else:
   1293             n = self._masked
-> 1294         return poisson_chi2(n, mu)
   1295 
   1296 

/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py in poisson_chi2(n, mu)
    345         n, mu = np.atleast_1d(n, mu)  # type:ignore
    346         if mu.dtype in (np.float32, np.float64):  # type:ignore
--> 347             return _poisson_chi2_nb(n, mu)
    348         # fallback to numpy for float128
    349         return _poisson_chi2_np(n, mu)

ValueError: unable to broadcast argument 1 to output array
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py", line 1, 
```
We need a better error message for this cost function and for others.

title: Supplying gradients for minimzation has no effect
body: Hello, I am experiencing the same issue as was discussed here: https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/issues/252 by @HDembinski. I have a custom function called NLL. I am trying to perform a fit using iminuit, but noticed that the execution time and performance were sub-optimal. Upon further checking, I found that the following command, where I pass the gradient of the NLL function explicitly to iminuit: ``` m1 = Minuit(jax.jit(NLL), (1.0,0.0), grad=jax.jit(jax.grad(NLL))) m1.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES m1.tol = 10.0 m1.strategy = 0 m1.migrad() ``` gives the exact same output with the same number of function calls, time of execution and fit values as not providing any gradient at all: ``` m1 = Minuit(jax.jit(NLL), (1.0,0.0), grad=None) m1.errordef = Minuit.LEAST_SQUARES m1.tol = 10.0 m1.strategy = 0 m1.migrad() ``` The output for both is given below: This remains true no matter which strategy I use (0, 1 or 2). I see in https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/issues/252 that the issue was resolved for v1.3. Has this been propagated to v2? I am currently using v2.16.0. Or am I doing something wrong? Thanks!

title: Move to scikit-build-core
body: @henryiii I also would like to switch to it when it becomes available.

title: `iminuit` fails to build because of `cmake-python-distributions` build dependency
body:
See scikit-build/cmake-python-distributions#324 which has my example of trying to install `iminuit`. 

The short story is `pip install iminuit` fails when to build when there's a lack of wheels. It seems the `cmake` "binary" from `cmake-python-distributions` fails to import its module, causing the whole build to fail. 

When "globally" installing `cmake-python-distributions` the build works fine, but then you have this problem, cmake-python-distributions/#80 (ie. c-p-d will overwrite CMake binaries).

When doing `pip wheel .` things obviously fail in the same way. I deleted the `cmake` dependency in `pyproject.toml` and relying on the system `cmake` things built as expected.

title: Provide macOS Monterey and Ventura wheels
body:
As I'm sure you're aware the Github actions runner image `macos-latest` still points to Big Sur. `macos-12` is the runner image for Monterey. Up-to-date wheels would be super helpful for `pip`ing in `iminuit` and prevent issues like #813. 

Unfortunately, as was unofficially announced just yesterday (https://github.com/actions/runner-images/issues/6426#issuecomment-1341047365), the Ventura image won't be available until Q2. I can provide a Ventura self-hosted runner in the meantime.

title: Specify required version of numpy
body:
iminuit is currently missing a version requirement on numpy but requires a relatively recent version.

This leads to import errors when pinning the numpy version to an older version, since pip and conda cannot know that newer versions of iminuit are not compatible.

The specific error I encountered is:

```
ImportError: cannot import name 'NDArray' from 'numpy.typing' (/home/mnoethe/.local/conda/envs/icrc2021-pipelines/lib/python3.8/site-packages/numpy/typing/__init__.py)
```

which was introduced in versions 1.21, so at this moment, this would be the minimum required numpy version iminuit needs to require.

title: [bug]: annotation evaluation breaks non-standard delayed annotation evalutation
body:
The added evaluation of delayed annotations (https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/develop/src/iminuit/util.py#L1313) only works with annotation from the Typing module and will fail with custom annotations (as they're not in the `Typing` namespace).

Example (this is a simplified example to illustrate the case! In this, *oversimplified*, example, we could simply remove the delayed annotation and bind the actual pandas DF. That's not the point, it's just to illustrate the problem with delayed annotations and different namespaces that we experience):
```
import pandas as pd  # this could be any library
from iminuit import cost

def f(x, mu: "pd.DataFrame"):
    return x * mu
    
nllfail = cost.UnbinnedNLL([3, 4], f)  # this fails with a name error as "pd" is not defined in the eval scope
```

title: FitResult may raise ImportError if matplotlib is not installed in Jupyter notebooks
body: The display of FitResult in a notebook calls `Minuit.visualize` since a few versions. If matplotlib is not installed, this fails, but it should instead raise a warning that visualization requires matplotlib.

title: Support Minuit.fixed[...] = number
body: Often we want to fix a parameter to some value. Instead of calling .fixed and .values, assigning a value to .fixed should do both.

title: Proof-read iminuit basic manual
body: The basic manual contains some typos. Check whether it is possible to do that automatically with a language model.

title: Improve Minuit.fmin._repr_html_ and repr
body:
How Hesse failures are displayed is confusing. If Hesse is not run, the display still says "Hesse Ok".

Instead we should have just one row with one or two fields. If Hesse was not run, just show one yellow bar with the message "Hesse not run". If Hesse failed, show this in red and what error was reported. If Hesse succeeded by forcing, make it yellow and show that. Likewise if the covariance is approximate. If Hesse succeeded and is accurate, show that in green and use the second field for accurate.

title: Cannot redirect output to text file
body:
On Windows, in Terminal, I can run the program

from iminuit import Minuit

def cost_function(x, y, z):

m = Minuit(cost_function, x=0, y=0, z=0)
m.migrad()  # run optimiser
m.hesse()   # run covariance estimator
print(str(m))

title: Suppression of Migrad output
body:
Hi, I am using `iMinuit` version 2.18.0. I am wondering if there is a way, or an implementation could be made, to suppress (all) output from `migrad` and similar fitting functions. For example, running `Minuit.migrad()` will produce an output along the lines of:


```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                Migrad                                   │
├──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ FCN = -0.06308                   │              Nfcn = 325              │
│ EDM = 7.41e-09 (Goal: 0.0002)    │                                      │
├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Valid Minimum           │       SOME Parameters at limit       │
├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Below EDM threshold (goal x 10)  │           Below call limit           │
├───────────────┬──────────────────┼───────────┬─────────────┬────────────┤
│  Covariance   │     Hesse ok     │ Accurate  │  Pos. def.  │ Not forced │
└───────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────┴─────────────┴────────────┘
┌───┬──────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────────┬────────────┬─────────┬─────────┬───────┐
│   │ Name │   Value   │ Hesse Err │ Minos Err- │ Minos Err+ │ Limit-  │ Limit+  │ Fixed │
├───┼──────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┼────────────┼─────────┼─────────┼───────┤
│ 0 │ ns   │    1e3    │    4e3    │            │            │    0    │1.12076e+09│       │
└───┴──────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────┴────────────┴─────────┴─────────┴───────┘
┌────┬──────────┐
│    │       ns │
├────┼──────────┤
│ ns │ 1.86e+07 │
└────┴──────────┘
```
I would like to suppress this output when performing a high number of minimizations as to avoid unnecessarily long outputs especially during interactive (i.e., command line or Jupyter Notebook) usage. Other ways of suppressing output such as [here ](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2125702/how-to-suppress-console-output-in-python) or [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2828953/silence-the-stdout-of-a-function-in-python-without-trashing-sys-stdout-and-resto) seem to not work (I guess the way that `iMinuit` displays the above output is not aligned with these suppression methods).

title: Error in iminuit.util.describe
body: When trying to run the code on generic least squares function page (https://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/stable/notebooks/generic_least_squares.html), there is an error trying to run the describe function. It is because there are two functions both called iminuit.util.describe (https://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference.html#iminuit.util.describe) and the generic least squares requires the version with 2 callable parameters which is being supressed by the version with 1 callable parameters.

    def interactive(
        self,

        raise_on_exception=False,
        **kwargs,
    ):
        """
        Return fitting widget (requires ipywidgets, IPython, matplotlib).
    
        A fitting widget is returned which can be displayed and manipulated in a
        Jupyter notebook to find good starting parameters and to debug the fit.
    
        Parameters
        ----------
        plot : Callable, optional
            To visualize the fit, interactive tries to access the visualize method on
            the cost function, which accepts the current model parameters as an
            array-like and potentially further keyword arguments, and draws a
            visualization into the current matplotlib axes. If the cost function does
            not provide a visualize method or if you want to override it, pass the
            function here.

            The default is to catch exceptions in the plot function and convert them
            into a plotted message. In unit tests, raise_on_exception should be set to
            True to allow detecting errors.
        **kwargs :
            Any other keyword arguments are forwarded to the plot function.
    
        Examples
        --------

            :include-source:

title: `_safe_log` is not safe
body:
I found that `_safe_log` is unfortunately not a safe operation in all cases. Only affected in architectures with bad floating point precision.

The follow logs are available:

- [i386 (aka x86 32-bit)](https://buildd.debian.org/status/fetch.php?pkg=iminuit&arch=arm64&ver=2.22.0-1&stamp=1688430799&raw=0): fails during `_ubinned_dll` (probably trying to sum `-inf` from `x=0`)
- [s390x](https://buildd.debian.org/status/fetch.php?pkg=iminuit&arch=s390x&ver=2.22.0-1&stamp=1688430563&raw=0): fails during `_unbinned_dll` in `test_UnbinnedNLL` (not quite clear to me where exactly this goes wrong)
- [mips64el](https://buildd.debian.org/status/fetch.php?pkg=iminuit&arch=mips64el&ver=2.22.0-1&stamp=1688432984&raw=0): fails during `poisson_chi2`
- [mipsel](https://buildd.debian.org/status/fetch.php?pkg=iminuit&arch=mipsel&ver=2.22.0-1&stamp=1688439302&raw=0): fails during `poisson_chi2`

What connects all of these failures is the fact that they call `_safe_log`. Notably, the implementation:
```python3
def _safe_log(x):
    # guard against x = 0
    return np.log(x + 1e-323)
```

`1e-323` was probably picked since it is close to `np.finfo(float).smallest_subnormal` (which would be the exact value).

However, this only works when working with 64-bit floats, `np.finfo(np.float32).smallest_subnormal` evaluates to `1e-45`. I think the issue is that during the addition when x is a 32-bit float, `1e-323` is casted to a 32-bit float for some reason. Because ` np.float32(1e-323)` evaluates to `0.0` the log returns `-inf`.

One way this could be tested more precisely is by checking the result of `_safe_log` in the tests is actually not `-inf` since `np.log(0.0)` does not throw any error (would need to be adjusted in `test_without_numba`).

I'm not entirely sure what the best solution is here - I see 3 options:
1. Instead of adding something to x, don't execute the log for values where x is zero: `np.log(x, where=(x>0.), out=np.full_like(x, np.log(np.finfo(float).smallest_subnormal)))`. Disadvantage: always returns array even for basic floats, potentially slow
2. Use the 32-bit smallest subnormal instead of the 64-bit one. Disadvantage: potential loss of precision.
3. Provide a `_safe_log` option that depends on the datatype and uses the appropriate smallest subnormal. However I'm not sure how this can be implemented.
4. Clip the `-inf`: `np.clip(np.log(x), -1e3, None)`. Disadvantage: slightly slower than current implementation probably.

I did a quick test of option 2 using the following code:
```python3
import numpy as np

_SSB32 = np.finfo(np.float32).smallest_subnormal
_SSB64 = np.finfo(np.float64).smallest_subnormal
_reasonably_small = 1e-40
_normal_small = 1e-30

def pretty(x):
  return np.format_float_scientific(x, exp_digits=3, precision=4, min_digits=4)

def logdiff(x):
  return np.log(x+_SSB32)-np.log(x)

print(f"""
For {pretty(_SSB64)}: {pretty(logdiff(_SSB64))}
For {pretty(_SSB32)}: {pretty(logdiff(_SSB32))}
For {pretty(_reasonably_small)}: {pretty(logdiff(_reasonably_small))}
For {pretty(_normal_small)}: {pretty(logdiff(_normal_small))}
""")
```
Output:
```
For 4.9407e-324: 6.4116e+002
For 1.4013e-045: 6.9315e-001
For 1.0000e-040: 1.4013e-005
For 1.0000e-030: 0.0000e+000
```

So I don't think it's entirely unreasonable, for more or less all number people should use (i.e. x>1e-30), the difference is precisely zero. However, for the current approach this limits is roughly 1e-310.

I think overall option 4 provides the best balance between precision, performance, ease of implementation and platform compatibility.

title: iminuit hangs when trying to visualize step function
body:
The following minimization seemingly hangs when running the following in Jupyter. @l-althueser did some investigations and found out that this does not occur when avoiding the visualization, e.g. by running this as a Python script or by calling `migrad` like `unused = m.migrad()` instead. It also hangs when just calling `m.visualize()` and when using a KeyboardInterrupt after that call, it occurs in `/usr/local/lib64/python3.10/site-packages/iminuit/util.py:1560, in _smart_sampling(f, xmin, xmax, start, tol)`.

Used `iminuit` version: `2.23.0`.

```
from iminuit import Minuit
from iminuit.cost import LeastSquares
import numpy as np

def model(x, c1):
    c2 = 100
    res = np.zeros(len(x))
    mask = x < 47
    res[mask] = c1
    res[~mask] = c2
    return res

xtest = np.linspace(0,74)
ytest = xtest*0+1
ytesterr = ytest

least_squares = LeastSquares(xtest, ytest, ytesterr, model)

m = Minuit(least_squares, c1=1)
m.print_level = 3
m.migrad()
#m.hesse()
```

title: Better documentation for errordef
body: Please document which cost functions require which values for errordef. For example, the ExtendedUnbinned cost function actually uses errordef=1, which was surprising to me since I was expecting it to act like a likelihood rather than a chi-square function (I actually think it's great, especially for beginners, that by default one does not need to set errordef for this). I would suggest adding a quick sentence to each cost function in the documentation telling people what to use for errordef (I looked at the actual implementation of the function to figure this out!)

\--> 207 return self.\_minimizer.profile(parameter\_name, low, high, sigma, cl, size, subtract\_min, arrows)

\--> 327 \_bins, \_vals, \_statuses \= self.\_\_iminuit.mnprofile(parameter\_name, \*\*\_kwargs)

File src/iminuit/\_libiminuit.pyx:1313, in iminuit.\_libiminuit.Minuit.mnprofile()

File /opt/conda/lib/python3.9/site-packages/numpy/\_\_init\_\_.py:324, in \_\_getattr\_\_(attr)
