```
\`np.bool\` was a deprecated alias for the builtin \`bool\`. To avoid this error in existing code, use \`bool\` by itself. Doing this will not modify any behavior and is safe. If you specifically wanted the numpy scalar type, use \`np.bool\_\` here.
The aliases was originally deprecated in NumPy 1.20; for more details and guidance see the original release note at:
    [https://numpy.org/devdocs/release/1.20.0-notes.html#deprecations](https://numpy.org/devdocs/release/1.20.0-notes.html#deprecations)
```

</details>

The problem seems to be that at some point `numpy.bool` gets called which then raises an error because it has been removed. The NumPy version on the machine where the error was encountered is 1.26, according to the [NumPy patch notes](https://numpy.org/devdocs/release/1.24.0-notes.html), the first affected NumPy version is 1.24. It seems to me that this is an issue within iminuit rather than our project, especially since we do not, at any point, call `numpy.bool`

title: Misleading documentation for iminuit.util.make_with_signature()
body:
The object returned by `make_with_signature()` cannot be called with keyword arguments. However the current documentation simply states:
> Return new callable with altered signature.
[...]
**Return type:**  callable with new argument names.

This leads to the expectation that the new callable behaves as if it had been defined with the new arguments. It’ll be helpful if the inability to take keyword arguments is mentioned explicitly.

title: Specifying relationships between fit parameters
body:
Apologies if I'm beating a dead horse here, or if I missed some obvious piece of documentation.

I'm new to iminuit and looking into how/whether it allows arbitrary constraints of fit parameters. If I understand correctly, I may [fix the value of a parameter to a constant floating number](https://iminuit.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference.html#iminuit.Minuit.values) using the `Minuit.fixed` property. Based on #638 it seems that constraining parameters as functions of other parameters (e.g., `param2 = 2*param1`) is not supported and will not be? Is that due implementation/complexity cost or because such a feature is considered out of scope? #638 seems to suggest the latter, but one could argue that fixing parameters to a function or to a constant are not conceptually different (although quite different to implement, granted).

FWIW I believe non-constant constraints on parameters is a killer feature in many use cases. Writing a bespoke cost function instead works when one is writing the whole code, but is not really an option when the end-user is using a library that they didn't write. In that case it's extremely powerful to provide simple hooks specifying relationships between parameters.

Whatever the case, thanks for your work on iminuit. It looks pretty powerful and I'll keep looking into it.

title: Bundled root version uses the imp module (removed with Python 3.12)
body:
Hi! :wave: 

I'm currently investigating upstream projects still using the `imp` module (which is [removed with Python 3.12](https://docs.python.org/dev/whatsnew/3.12.html#imp)), as we are about to upgrade to Python 3.12 on Arch Linux.

This project uses https://github.com/HDembinski/root/tree/503faf859b51c337dbc05c44a0c383f1ea5c1402 as submodule and root still makes use of the `imp` module:

https://github.com/root-project/root/issues/14824

In case this poses a problem for building or running this project (it didn't seem to cause issues building when looking at #960), I would like to establish this ticket for tracking purposes (and to link to the upstream ticket with root that I created).

Thank you for your work :bow:

title: cost.LeastSquares fails with data with more than 2 dimensions
body:
For multidimensional x data formatted as a DxN array as described in the documentation, any D greater than 2 will cause iminuit to fail with the following error:
```
File "/usr/lib/python3.11/site-packages/iminuit/cost.py", line 1721, in _call
    y, yerror = self._masked.T[self._ndim :]
    ^^^^^^^^^
ValueError: too many values to unpack (expected 2)
```

See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/76127508/iminuit-high-dimension-multivariate-fit-problem

I believe this is because the following line assumes that x.ndim gives D:
https://github.com/scikit-hep/iminuit/blob/298afadc645999fbad32ca00be6c0ebfec7c7bb9/src/iminuit/cost.py#L2194

However, with correctly formatted data, `x.ndim` can only give 1 or 2, and D is instead given by `np.atleast_2d(x).shape[0]` or simply `x.shape[0]` if the `atleast_2d` call that is already in the method is moved earlier.

title: Update to trusted publisher releases
body:
All stored secreted were cleared by the Scikit-HEP team due to a security threat that turned out to be a GitHub bug. It is recommended that Scikit-HEP projects move to trusted publisher releases instead of regenerating tokens. This requires telling PyPI about the GitHub details (repo location, ci job file, and environment), and setting up something like this (taken from https://learn.scientific-python.org/development/guides/gha-wheels/#publishing):

```yaml
upload_all:
  needs: [build_wheels, make_sdist]
  environment: pypi
  permissions:
    id-token: write
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  if: github.event_name == 'release' && github.event.action == 'published'
  steps:
    - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
      with:
        pattern: cibw-*
        path: dist
        merge-multiple: true

    - uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1
```

I'm not sure how this interacts with branch-based releases that are currently used. I think you might be able to just remove the "environment" field (and leave the field blank on PyPI) and it might just work (though be less secure and have fewer options for release approvals and such that GitHub environments provide).

title: Error: IMinuitWarning: MIGRAD fails to converge
body:
Hello guys,

I am using Minuit for a likelihood analysis. It seems that after a value of a parameter "x" the function could degenerate and we expect a sharp decrease in vertical way for the parameter "x=limit_value", but this not happens and the values of "y" remain constant at the value "y(limit_value)=y_limit". Why this happens? The error that I have is "IMinuitWarning: MIGRAD fails to converge for y=###"

You need all script to better understand?
Thanks

title: Add citation information to Software Citation Station?
body:
Hi! Someone recently added a package to the Software Citation Station that depends on your package. We don't currently have your package listed but would love for it to be shown on our site.

The Software Citation Station is a simple web tool for quickly generating the necessary LaTeX/BibTeX to properly acknowledge the software used in research and ensure work is properly credited.

- [Add your software with this form](https://www.tomwagg.com/software-citation-station/?new-software=true)
- [Main website](https://www.tomwagg.com/software-citation-station/)
- [Read more in our paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.04405)

title: Deployment of iminuit 2.26 on conda
body:
So far the release of iminuit 2.26 on conda-forge is not done.  As it is a required dependency for gammapy, this will cause issues for future releases supporting numpy 2.0. 
Is there a plan to deploy on conda?

title: Pass `ncall` and `iterate` in methods that build profile likelihoods
body:
Hi @HDembinski,

Would you be open to allow `migrad` optional arguments as arguments to method that return profile likelihoods like `mnprofile`. I'm getting the warning that migrad failed to converge quite often in such cases and I thought this might be helpful.
I could make a PR, just asking if you'd be open to it.

title: Support for Plotly or Seanborn?
body: It's great to have interactive plots when fitting, so is there any plan for that?

title: [interactive fit]: huge & tiny value is not displayed properly on sliders
body:
For example, my limits are listed below.
```python
m_RSq = Minuit(loss_RSq, *initial_params_RSq)
m = m_RSq
m.limits["scale"] = (1e15, 1e20)
m.limits["Ga"] = (1e-18, 1e-16)
m.limits["Rga"] = (1, 200)
m.limits["Ba"] = (1e-18, 1e-16)
```
I do interactive fitting after that.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/de1d3ed0-316b-4744-b939-f953ddf0d5a5)

Both major and small values, in my opinion, should be presented in a scientific manner to make them easier to adjust.

title: feat: include bounds widgets in the interactive fit along with a slider
body: If we could set boundaries interactively, that would be great.

title: Python 3.13 wheels
body: Python 3.13 comes out on ~~Tuesday~~ (week from Monday, emergency revert on the incremental GC), and most of the rest of Scikit-HEP is ready for it. iMinuit is missing wheels for 3.13 (and 3.13t, ideally). I think it's just missing from the CI matrix, deps have been ready for a month or more.

title: `NormalConstraint` is missing from API reference
body: For some reason, the `NormalConstraint` cost function is missing from the API reference (https://scikit-hep.org/iminuit/reference.html#).

title: can't unbox array from PyObject into native value error
body:
Hello, this example is from the tutorial

```
from iminuit.cost import LeastSquares
from iminuit import Minuit

def line(x, α, β):
    return α + x * β


# generate random toy data with random offsets in y
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
data_x = np.linspace(0, 1, 10)
data_yerr = 0.1  # could also be an array
data_y = rng.normal(line(data_x, 1, 2), data_yerr)

least_squares = LeastSquares(data_x, data_y, data_yerr, line)

m = Minuit(least_squares, α=0, β=0)  # starting values for α and β

m.migrad()  # finds minimum of least_squares function
m.hesse()  # accurately computes uncertainties
```

I get `TypeError: can't unbox array from PyObject into native value.  The object maybe of a different type`

title: plots don't obey limits
body:
After running
```
m_fldaCDM.migrad()
m_fldaCDM.hesse() 
```
in a Jupyter notebook, the plots of data and fit are automatically displayed, but the fitted line is only plotted over the first and last datapoint. In an unsorted array/list, this gives unexpected plots, not starting from the beginning. A simple addition of min/max calls when plotting would solve this issue.

title: 403 on link in LICENSE file
body: Noting that the http://seal.web.cern.ch/seal/main/license.html link in the LICENSE file is returning 403 Forbidden.

title: Confidence level docstring for minos
body:
The docstring for `cl` in [`minos`](https://scikit-hep.org/iminuit/reference.html#iminuit.Minuit.minos) is

> **cl** (_float or None, optional_) – Confidence level of the interval. If not set or None, a standard 68 % interval is computed (default). [...]

which to me sounds like it would by default compute a 68.00% confidence interval. It does however also say "standard" which might have different meanings to different people. The current behavior is that the default is equivalent to `cl=1`, corresponding to a 68.27% interval instead.

I propose to change the docstring to "68.27 %" to clarify this, happy to submit a PR if useful.

title: Improving the Qt Widget
body: @adryyan added a nice Qt Widget for interactive fitting in #1056. Unfortunately, the layout is off when using it on Windows (assuming it was correct on Linux). It could be that the layout in Qt is not perfectly platform-independent. **The layout of this plot is also broken, but this has nothing to do with Qt and will be fixed in a separate PR.** - [ ] Fix the space requirement of the Parameter widget - [ ] Add handles so that the user can interactively change the size of the widgets inside the window

title: how to make use of user-provided analytical second-order gradient function
body: even providing a analytical first-order gradient function, Minuit does benefit much because it has to use NumericalGradient calculator for second-order gradient. I hope there a way that user-provided analytical second-order gradient function speed the optimizing process.

title: possible to create new release?
body: Would it be possible to create a new release to so that the changes in #1060 can be pip installed? We are releasing some code to our collaboration that relies on #1060 and it would be easier if they could just pip install the package rather than clone the develop branch.

title: [Feature Request] Allow disabling of `pdg_format`
body:
Hi!

When inspecting some logs, I was suprised to see `m.values` and `m.params` return rather different values for the same object, namely a parameter with a `__repr__` of

```
Param(number=2, name='klambda_ttH120200', value=1.0, error=41.69716021513093, merror=None, is_const=False, is_fixed=False, lower_limit=None, upper_limit=None)
```
displaying as 
```
┌───┬───────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────────┬────────────┬─────────┬─────────┬───────┐
│   │ Name              │   Value   │ Hesse Err │ Minos Err- │ Minos Err+ │ Limit-  │ Limit+  │ Fixed │
├───┼───────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┼────────────┼─────────┼─────────┼───────┤
│ 0 │ klambda_ttH120200 │     0     │    40     │            │            │         │         │       │
└───┴───────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────┴────────────┴─────────┴─────────┴───────┘
```
when printed. 

After some digging I think this is related to the `_round()` function as part of `pdg_format`. Whilst I agree that if one follows the PDG Guidelines then this is the correct representation of this parameter given the large error, I don't think it's the *most* helpful thing someone could see. Seeing this value was particularly surprising given this was for an Asimov fit and so one expects to see a value of `1`.

I know the simple solution would be to just "use `.values()` if you actually want the values", but I find the table format one gets with ".params()" much easier to read!

Happy to propose changes in a PR if this is something you think would provide value, thanks :)

title: show with yellow color if a parameter is at limit, in jupyter
`iminuit` and other related projects' maintainership in the future
body:
Hi @HDembinski, 

I'm opening an issue here because as far as I understand `iminuit` is your "main" project that you still actively maintain while not being in physics anymore. I would have sent this in an email but I don't know your personal email and I don't know if any of the public institutional ones work anymore.

I would like to ask, what are your plans regarding maintaining the projects that you've been leading so far? I notice that you're actively maintaining `iminuit` which is critical in the pythonic HEP ecosystem but have you considered having someone else (probably from IRIS-HEP) assist in that role?

Also there are other related projects that are very nice additions to people using `iminuit` like `numba-stats` and `jacobi`. Those are under your personal account and not `scikit-hep` and it would be really nice if they can have some attention from someone else and continue to exist.

Finally, there is a big elephant in the room as well which is `boost/histogram`. I think it would be nice if that has a second appointed maintainer as well. Perhaps @henryiii ?

I fully understand that its not your job and not your responsibility to maintain any of these projects and all of us in the pythonic HEP community are grateful that you do. I'm just trying to get some insight and see how we can (people related to IRIS-HEP/scikit-hep) can help 😃  (perhaps relevant to the upcoming PyHEPdev workshop that we're gonna have in July).

Kind regards,
Iason.

title: [Feature Request] Allow user to supply G2
body: Allow the user to supply MINUIT with an externally computed 2nd derivative of the gradient (e.g., with auto diff), in the same way that the user may specify the grad.

title: Regression in 2.31.2: AttributeError raised from `_repr_html_`
body:
MWE:

```python

from iminuit import Minuit
from iminuit.cost import LeastSquares
import numpy as np


def line(x, α, β):
    return α + x * β


rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
data_x = np.linspace(0, 1, 10)
data_yerr = 0.1
data_y = rng.normal(line(data_x, 1, 2), data_yerr)
least_squares = LeastSquares(data_x, data_y, data_yerr, line)

m = Minuit(least_squares, α=0, β=0)

m.migrad()
m.minos()

m.limits = [(0, None), (0, 10)]
m.params._repr_html_()
```


This raises `AttributeError: 'tuple' object has no attribute 'lower'`.

Commenting out `m.minos()` no longer raises the error.

Looks like it originated from #1092. Seems like running `.minos()` turns the `.merror` attribute into a plain python tuple?

title: typing Cost
body:
Hi, I noticed that
1. Pyright throws a warning that it can't find the stub file for the packgage; perhaps add the "py.typed" file in the package root?
2. the Cost is typed as a run-time checkable protocol that currently does nothing... the run-time check can be performed by doing a `callable()` call on the passed function, and the variadic signature `*args, **kwargs` does not accept non-variadic cost functions, at least in "full" type checking mode.

not a significant issue but annoying for anyone who wants "full" typing, having to spam `ignore` comments
I would be happy contribute a pull request some time this year.

what is the minimum python version the package has to support? so I know which typing features are usable

cheers
